## The Impact of Overfishing on Marine Ecosystems

[**Author info »**](#ai)

#### About the Study

Overfishing is a significant problem that has been affecting marine ecosystems around the world. It is the act of catching too many fish, to the point where the fish population cannot replenish itself at a sustainable rate. It has far-reaching effects on not only the fish population but also the entire marine ecosystem and the people who rely on it for their livelihood. One of the immediate effects of overfishing is the decline in fish populations. When too many fish are caught, the fish population cannot reproduce fast enough to keep up with the demand. This leads to a reduction in the number of fish in the ocean, which affects not only the fish but also other marine [species](https://www.longdom.org/peer-reviewed-journals/species-5796.html) that depend on them for food. This depletion of fish populations has a domino effect on the ecosystem, ultimately leading to an imbalance that affects the entire food chain. Overfishing can also have a significant impact on the marine environment. When fish populations are depleted, other [species](https://www.longdom.org/peer-reviewed-journals/species-5796.html) in the food chain that rely on those fish for food are affected as well. For example, seabirds, dolphins, and whales depend on fish for their survival. When fish populations decline, these animals may migrate to new areas in search of food, disrupting the balance of the ecosystem. Overfishing can also lead to an extinction of certain fish species. Some fish [species](https://www.longdom.org/peer-reviewed-journals/species-5796.html) have been overfished to the point where their populations have declined so significantly that they are in danger of extinction. This is not only a loss for the ecosystem but also for the people who depend on those fish [species](https://www.longdom.org/peer-reviewed-journals/species-5796.html) for their livelihood. For example, certain types of tuna are highly valued in the [fishing](https://www.longdom.org/peer-reviewed-journals/fishing-1291.html) industry, and their overfishing can lead to a significant economic impact. The economic impact of overfishing can be devastating for many communities that rely on [fishing](https://www.longdom.org/peer-reviewed-journals/fishing-1291.html) as their primary source of income. When fish populations decline, fishermen may have to travel further and spend more time and money to catch the same amount of fish. This can lead to a decrease in their income and the loss of jobs. In addition, the cost of fish can increase, making it difficult for low-income families to afford this source of protein. Overfishing can also have a significant impact on the cultural traditions of communities that rely on fishing. [Fishing](https://www.longdom.org/peer-reviewed-journals/fishing-1291.html) has been a way of life for many communities for generations. When fish populations decline, it not only affects their livelihood but also their cultural identity. This loss can have a significant psychological impact on these communities, leading to feelings of sadness and hopelessness. One of the most significant long-term effects of overfishing is the damage to the marine ecosystem. When fish populations decline, the entire ecosystem can be disrupted. For example, overfishing of a certain [species](https://www.longdom.org/peer-reviewed-journals/species-5796.html) of fish can lead to an increase in the population of their predators. This can have a cascading effect on the entire food chain, ultimately leading to a decline in the health of the entire ecosystem. This can also lead to the loss of important marine habitats, such as coral reefs, which provide shelter and breeding grounds for many marine species. Overfishing can also lead to the spread of disease in marine populations. When fish populations are stressed due to overfishing, they become more susceptible to disease. This can lead to the spread of diseases to other marine species, ultimately leading to the decline of entire populations. Addressing overfishing is crucial to the health of the marine ecosystem and the people who depend on it. One solution is to establish sustainable [fishing](https://www.longdom.org/peer-reviewed-journals/fishing-1291.html) practices. This includes limiting the amount of fish that can be caught, regulating [fishing](https://www.longdom.org/peer-reviewed-journals/fishing-1291.html) methods, and protecting important habitats. This can help ensure that fish populations can replenish themselves at a sustainable rate, while also protecting the ecosystem as a whole.

# Overfishing: Depleting Our Oceans at an Alarming Rate

# **The Urgent Need to Address Overfishing for Ocean Conservation**

[**Overfishing**](https://www.msc.org/what-we-are-doing/oceans-at-risk/overfishing) has emerged as one of the most pressing environmental issues of our time, posing a serious threat to marine biodiversity and the health of our oceans. This phenomenon occurs when fish are caught at a rate faster than they can reproduce, leading to a drastic decline in fish populations. As a result, overfishing not only endangers specific species but also disrupts entire marine ecosystems, affecting the delicate balance that sustains life underwater.  
  
Historically, fishing has been a vital source of food and livelihood for millions of people worldwide. However, the advent of industrial fishing practices, combined with illegal, unreported, and unregulated [**(IUU) fishing**](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/insight/understanding-illegal-unreported-and-unregulated-fishing), has significantly exacerbated the problem. The demand for seafood continues to rise, driven by population growth and economic pressures, pushing global fish stocks to the brink of collapse. Today, [**approximately 90% of the world’s fish stocks are fully exploited, overexploited, or depleted**](https://unctad.org/news/90-fish-stocks-are-used-fisheries-subsidies-must-stop)**.**

The consequences of overfishing are far-reaching. Marine ecosystems suffer as key species disappear, leading to the loss of biodiversity and the disruption of food chains. Coastal communities that rely on fishing for their economic survival face declining catches and increasing poverty. Additionally, the broader environmental implications include the potential for irreversible damage to ocean health and the services it provides, such as carbon sequestration and climate regulation.

## **Understanding Overfishing**

Overfishing is a significant issue that goes beyond simply depleting fish populations. It [**disrupts the intricate balance of marine ecosystems**](https://californianorth.com/uncategorized/how-overfishing-destroys-our-oceans/), leading to cascading effects that threaten marine biodiversity. This phenomenon occurs when fish are harvested at rates too high for species to maintain healthy populations. Unlike traditional fishing methods that allowed for natural replenishment, modern industrial fishing practices have led to unsustainable extraction levels.  
  
The history of overfishing can be traced back to the mid-20th century, a period marked by technological advancements in fishing equipment and the expansion of global markets. Techniques such as bottom trawling and longlining enabled fishermen to capture vast quantities of fish, far outstripping the rate at which fish could reproduce. This period also saw the rise of illegal, unreported, and unregulated (IUU) fishing, which further exacerbated the depletion of fish stocks.  
  
The statistics are alarming: the [**FAO reports that 34.2% of fish stocks are overfished, and an additional 59.6% are fished at their maximum sustainable levels**.](https://www.fao.org/3/cc0461en/online/sofia/2022/status-of-fishery-resources.html) This leaves almost 90% of the world’s fish stocks at risk of depletion. Species like the Atlantic cod and the Pacific bluefin tuna have seen their populations plummet due to overfishing, with the latter experiencing a decline of over 90%.  
  
Beyond the numbers, the ecological impacts are profound. Overfishing leads to the loss of key species that play critical roles in marine ecosystems. The removal of predator species can result in the overpopulation of prey species, which in turn affects the entire food web. Moreover, bycatch – the incidental capture of non-target species such as dolphins, turtles, and seabirds – adds to the ecological strain, pushing many vulnerable species toward endangerment.  
  
Understanding overfishing requires recognizing it as a complex issue with deep historical roots and wide-reaching consequences. Addressing it involves not only changing fishing practices but also rethinking our relationship with the ocean and its resources.

The impact of overfishing on marine ecosystems is profound and multifaceted. To mitigate these effects, it is essential to implement sustainable fishing practices, protect critical habitats, and enforce regulations that limit bycatch and habitat destruction. By taking these steps, we can help restore balance to marine ecosystems and ensure the long-term health of our oceans.

## **Overfishing’s Long-term Consequences**

The long-term consequences of overfishing highlight the urgent need for sustainable fishing practices and effective management of fish stocks. By protecting and restoring marine ecosystems, we can ensure the continued availability of marine resources and the health of our oceans for future generations.

Overfishing is rapidly depleting our oceans, with devastating consequences for marine ecosystems and the health of our planet. The relentless pursuit of fish to feed a growing global population has pushed many species to the brink of collapse, disrupting delicate food webs and threatening the very survival of countless marine organisms. As industrial fishing fleets scour the seas with ever more sophisticated technology, they leave behind a wake of destruction – bycatch, damaged habitats, and ghostly underwater deserts devoid of life. But the impacts of overfishing extend far beyond the oceans themselves. Healthy marine ecosystems play a vital role in regulating the Earth’s climate, absorbing carbon dioxide and producing oxygen. The loss of [marine biodiversity](https://www.marinebiodiversity.ca/2024/09/5-reasons-marine-biodiversity-is-vital-for-life-on-earth/) undermines the ocean’s ability to perform these critical functions, exacerbating the effects of climate change on a global scale. Coastal communities that depend on fishing for their livelihoods and food security are also paying a heavy price, as dwindling fish stocks threaten their way of life and cultural heritage. It is clear that overfishing is not just a problem for the oceans – it is a problem for all of us. As we stand at this critical juncture, it is imperative that we take swift and decisive action to end overfishing and allow our marine ecosystems to recover. The future of our planet depends on it.

Overfishing 101: Understanding the Problem
------------------------------------------

### Defining Overfishing

Overfishing occurs when fish are caught faster than they can reproduce, leading to a decline in their population. This unsustainable practice is often driven by high demand, inadequate regulations, and illegal fishing activities. When a species is overfished, it becomes difficult for the population to recover, disrupting the delicate balance of marine ecosystems. Related terms include “bycatch,” which refers to the unintended capture of non-target species, and “ghost fishing,” where abandoned or lost fishing gear continues to trap and kill marine life. These practices contribute to the overexploitation of fish stocks and the destruction of marine habitats. Overfishing not only affects the targeted species but also has cascading effects on the entire ecosystem. As key species are removed, the food web is altered, leading to changes in species composition and the overall health of the marine environment. Understanding the concept of overfishing is crucial for recognizing its far-reaching impacts and the urgent need for sustainable fishing practices.

### The Scale and Severity of Overfishing Today

The scale and severity of overfishing have reached unprecedented levels in recent decades. According to the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), over 34% of global fish stocks are overfished, and another 60% are fished to their maximum sustainable limits. This means that nearly all commercially exploited fish populations are under immense pressure, with many on the brink of collapse. Overfishing has led to the rapid decline of iconic species like Atlantic bluefin tuna, whose population has plummeted by over 80% since the 1970s due to high demand and unsustainable fishing practices. Similarly, the once-abundant cod fisheries of the North Atlantic have been pushed to the point of commercial extinction, with devastating impacts on local communities and ecosystems. The situation is particularly dire in developing nations, where artisanal fisheries are a critical source of food and livelihood for millions. In West Africa, for instance, foreign industrial trawlers have decimated local fish populations, leading to food insecurity and economic hardship for coastal communities. The consequences of overfishing extend far beyond individual species. As key predators and prey are removed from the ecosystem, the delicate balance of marine food webs is disrupted, leading to cascading effects on biodiversity and ecosystem functioning. This, in turn, compromises the ocean’s ability to provide essential services like carbon sequestration, nutrient cycling, and coastal protection. The urgency of the overfishing crisis cannot be overstated. Without swift and decisive action to curb unsustainable fishing practices and promote responsible stewardship of our ocean resources, we risk irreversible damage to marine ecosystems and the countless species and communities that depend on them.

![Image 3: Commercial fishing boat with full nets, including non-target species as bycatch](https://www.marinebiodiversity.ca/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/commercial-fishing-trawler-bycatch.jpg)

A large commercial fishing trawler pulling up a net full of fish, with bycatch visible

The Ripple Effects of Overfishing
---------------------------------

### Disrupting the Food Web

Overfishing not only depletes targeted fish populations but also disrupts the delicate balance of marine ecosystems. When a species is overfished, it can no longer fulfill its ecological role, leading to a cascade of effects throughout the food web. For example, if a key predator is removed, its prey species may explode in numbers, potentially overgrazing on lower trophic levels and altering habitat structures. Conversely, overfishing prey species can deprive predators of their food source, causing their populations to decline. These imbalances can ripple through the entire ecosystem, affecting species that may not be directly targeted by fishing activities. Moreover, many commercial fishing methods, such as bottom trawling, can indiscriminately capture and kill non-target species, including endangered or protected animals like sea turtles, dolphins, and seabirds. This bycatch further disrupts the food web and can push vulnerable species closer to extinction. As marine ecosystems are highly interconnected, the loss of even a single species can have far-reaching consequences on the overall health and resilience of the ocean. To protect the intricate balance of marine life, it is crucial that we adopt sustainable fishing practices and allow overfished populations to recover.

![Image 4: Infographic depicting the impact of overfishing on the marine food web](https://www.marinebiodiversity.ca/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/overfishing-food-web-infographic.jpg)

An infographic illustrating the marine food web and how overfishing disrupts the balance

### Bycatch and Habitat Destruction

Overfishing often employs nonselective fishing methods, such as bottom trawling and driftnets, which indiscriminately capture marine life and damage delicate habitats. These destructive practices lead to high levels of bycatch—the unintended capture of non-target species, including dolphins, sea turtles, sharks, and juvenile fish. Bycatch not only decimates populations of these creatures but also disrupts the balance of marine ecosystems. Moreover, bottom trawling involves dragging heavy nets along the seafloor, destroying vital habitats like coral reefs, seagrass beds, and seamounts that provide shelter and breeding grounds for countless species. The loss of these habitats reduces biodiversity and undermines the ocean’s resilience to other stressors like climate change. Adopting selective fishing gear, establishing marine protected areas, and supporting sustainable fisheries are crucial steps in mitigating the devastating impacts of bycatch and habitat destruction. As renowned marine biologist Sylvia Earle once said, “No water, no life. No blue, no green.” It is our collective responsibility to protect the ocean’s delicate ecosystems and the myriad life forms they support.

![Image 5: Traditional fishing village showcasing the importance of fishing for local livelihoods](https://www.marinebiodiversity.ca/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/coastal-fishing-village.jpg)

A coastal fishing village with boats docked and fish drying racks, highlighting the community’s dependence on fishing

### Socioeconomic Impacts on Coastal Communities

Overfishing not only threatens marine ecosystems but also jeopardizes the livelihoods of coastal communities worldwide. Many of these communities heavily rely on fishing for their income and sustenance. As fish populations decline due to unsustainable fishing practices, small-scale fishers struggle to make ends meet. They often lack the resources to compete with large, industrialized fishing vessels that can travel farther and catch more fish. This leads to a vicious cycle of poverty, as fishers resort to using more aggressive and destructive methods to maximize their catch, further exacerbating the problem of overfishing. Moreover, the depletion of fish stocks can have ripple effects throughout the local economy. Fishing villages may experience a decline in tourism, as recreational fishing and diving become less attractive due to the lack of marine life. The processing and sale of fish products may also suffer, leading to job losses in related industries. Consequently, the socioeconomic fabric of these communities begins to unravel, forcing many residents to seek alternative livelihoods or migrate to urban areas in search of work. To protect both the marine environment and the well-being of coastal communities, it is crucial to promote sustainable fishing practices and support initiatives that help fishers transition to more responsible and resilient livelihoods.

The Path Forward: Solutions and Action Steps
--------------------------------------------

### Sustainable Fisheries Management

Sustainable fisheries management is crucial for maintaining healthy fish populations and preserving marine biodiversity. Governments, organizations, and communities worldwide have implemented successful policies and practices to promote responsible fishing. One effective approach is setting science-based catch limits that allow fish stocks to recover and thrive. These limits consider factors such as species’ reproductive rates, ecosystem dynamics, and climate change impacts. Quotas are allocated to fisheries based on these sustainable catch levels, ensuring long-term viability. Another key strategy is reducing bycatch—the unintended capture of non-target species. Innovative fishing gear modifications, such as turtle excluder devices and circle hooks, help minimize bycatch and protect vulnerable marine life. Temporal and spatial fishing restrictions, like marine protected areas and seasonal closures during spawning periods, give fish populations a chance to recover and reproduce. Collaborative efforts between fisheries, scientists, and conservationists have yielded promising results. Fishery improvement projects bring together stakeholders to assess fish stocks, implement sustainable practices, and achieve eco-certification. Consumer awareness campaigns, such as seafood guides and eco-labels, empower individuals to make informed choices and support sustainably managed fisheries. Governments play a vital role by enacting and enforcing regulations, monitoring compliance, and investing in research and monitoring. International agreements, like the UN Fish Stocks Agreement, foster cooperation among nations to manage shared fish stocks responsibly. By prioritizing long-term sustainability over short-term gains, these [conservation strategies](https://www.marinebiodiversity.ca/2019/03/oceans-work/) offer hope for the future of our oceans and the countless species that depend on them.

### How You Can Help

You can make a difference in protecting our oceans and marine life from the devastating impacts of overfishing. One of the most effective ways to support sustainable seafood is by making informed choices as a consumer. Opt for seafood certified by reputable organizations like the Marine Stewardship Council (MSC) or Aquaculture Stewardship Council (ASC), which ensure that the fish you purchase comes from responsibly managed fisheries or farms. Additionally, consult sustainable seafood guides, such as those provided by the Monterey Bay Aquarium’s Seafood Watch program, to help you make ocean-friendly choices at restaurants and grocery stores. Another way to contribute is by supporting organizations dedicated to marine conservation and sustainable fishing practices. Consider donating to or volunteering with groups like Oceana, the Ocean Conservancy, or local marine conservation initiatives in your area. These organizations work tirelessly to protect marine ecosystems, advocate for responsible fishing policies, and educate the public about the importance of ocean health. You can also make your voice heard by engaging with your elected officials and encouraging them to prioritize marine conservation and sustainable fishing practices in legislation and policy decisions. Write letters, attend town hall meetings, and participate in campaigns that call for stronger regulations and enforcement of fishing activities. Finally, spread awareness about the impacts of overfishing and the importance of sustainable seafood among your friends, family, and community. Share informative articles, host screenings of documentaries like “Seaspiracy” or “The End of the Line,” and encourage others to make responsible choices when it comes to seafood consumption. By collectively taking action and making conscious decisions, we can work towards restoring the health and abundance of our oceans for generations to come.

Conclusion
----------

Overfishing is a global crisis that demands immediate action to protect the health and biodiversity of our oceans. The devastating impacts of unsustainable fishing practices extend far beyond the targeted species, disrupting entire ecosystems and threatening the livelihoods of millions who depend on the sea. It is crucial that we recognize the urgency of this issue and take collective action to promote responsible fishing practices, support marine protected areas, and make informed choices as consumers. By [protecting our oceans](https://www.marinebiodiversity.ca/2024/09/exploring-the-wonders-of-marine-biodiversity-why-our-oceans-need-your-help/) and the incredible diversity of life they sustain, we can ensure a thriving future for generations to come. The time to act is now – together, we have the power to create positive change and restore the balance of our precious marine ecosystems.

### Weston Goyette

Weston Goyette is a passionate advocate for marine conservation and sustainability. With a background in marine biology and environmental science, Weston has dedicated his career to exploring the intricate relationships within ocean ecosystems and promoting strategies to protect them. At the Marine Biodiversity & Sustainability Learning Center, Weston contributes insightful articles that delve into topics such as endangered species protection, climate change impacts on marine life, and sustainable practices for preserving our oceans. His writings aim to educate and inspire readers to take actionable steps toward environmental stewardship. Beyond his written work, Weston actively participates in community outreach programs, engaging with local schools and organizations to raise awareness about marine conservation. His commitment to both scientific research and public education makes him a valuable asset to the Center's mission.

--- SOURCE 5: What Is Overfishing | Marine Stewardship Council ---
URL: https://www.msc.org/what-we-are-doing/oceans-at-risk/overfishing

SUMMARY:
Biodiversity impacts can take place not only through overfishing of fish stocks targeted for food, but also through overfishing of species caught unintentionally. As well as fishing healthy populations, fisheries must show they are managing their impacts on habitats and other marine species. What is the MSC doing about overfishing? The MSC Fisheries Standard is a set of requirements that ensure fisheries aiming to be certified as environmentally sustainable only target stocks that are abundant and able to replenish themselves, and that fisheries minimise their impact on the wider marine ecosystem. Recent MSC research found that fish stocks targeted by fisheries certified to the MSC Standard perform better on key sustainability benchmarks than those targeted by non-certified fisheries.

Overfishing
===========

*   [Home](https://www.msc.org/)
*   [Oceans at risk](https://www.msc.org/what-we-are-doing/oceans-at-risk)
*   What is overfishing?

Overfishing is a serious global problem that threatens ocean wildlife and biodiversity. Loss of marine life brings a further threat to the millions of people who rely on seafood for protein and livelihoods.

What is overfishing and why is it a problem?
--------------------------------------------

Overfishing occurs when too many fish in a particular stock are caught and there are not enough adults to breed and sustain a healthy population.

The level of overfishing has been increasing in recent decades and the number of overfished stocks is now three times higher than in 1970. The United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization monitors over 500 fish stocks around the globe. In 2024, [it was estimated that over 37% of these fish stocks were overfished.](https://openknowledge.fao.org/items/06690fd0-d133-424c-9673-1849e414543d)

[For communities reliant on fishing,](https://www.msc.org/what-we-are-doing/oceans-at-risk/the-impact-on-communities) the impact of stock collapses can be devastating. Overfishing is also a concern for the wild marine environment as it is one of the major causes of the loss of ocean biodiversity.

---------------------

What is driving overfishing?
----------------------------

There are several factors that drive overfishing, these include:

*   **Rising consumption**: The human population reached 8 billion in November 2022 and is [expected to reach almost 10 billion](https://www.un.org/development/desa/pd/sites/www.un.org.development.desa.pd/files/wpp2022_summary_of_results.pdf) by 2050. [Consumption of aquatic foods (farmed and wild) is rising twice as fast as the global population.](https://www.fao.org/newsroom/detail/record-fisheries-aquaculture-production-contributes-food-security-290622/en)
*   [**Climate change**:](https://www.msc.org/what-we-are-doing/oceans-at-risk/climate-change-and-fishing) Increases in ocean temperatures change the migratory routes of fish stocks, causing them to move away from traditional fishing grounds. Overfishing occurs when fisheries continue to catch at the same level even though some of their target species have moved to new grounds.
*   [**Illegal, Unreported and Unregulated (IUU) fishing**:](https://www.msc.org/what-we-are-doing/oceans-at-risk/illegal-unreported-unregulated-iuu-destructive-fishing) Catching fish without abiding to governmental and international regulations, for example in protected zones, or not reporting part of the catches.
*   **Fishing subsidies**: It is estimated that [governments have spent billions of dollars on subsidies to support their fishing industries.](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11625-020-00865-z) Sometimes, this might support fishing in overexploited areas where catch revenue will not be enough to cover the expense of fishing. In such cases subsidies end up encouraging unsustainable fishing practices.

[What is sustainable fishing?](https://www.msc.org/what-we-are-doing/our-approach/what-is-sustainable-fishing)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Fishing is sustainable if it leaves enough fish in the oceans and minimises impacts on habitats and ecosystems. For this to happen, fisheries must be managed effectively.

[![Image 7: Climate change and fishing](https://www.msc.org/ResourcePackages/MSC/assets/img/defaultListImg.jpg)](https://www.msc.org/what-we-are-doing/our-approach/what-is-sustainable-fishing)

What are the consequences of overfishing?
-----------------------------------------

[Almost 38 million people are directly employed in the wild capture seafood sector.](https://www.fao.org/3/cc0461en/cc0461en.pdf) Figures from the 2020 UN FAO State of the World Fisheries Report show 10% of the world’s population rely, at least partially, on fishing and aquaculture for their livelihoods.

If fish stocks collapse the impact can be immediate, but recovery can take decades. The collapse of Canada’s Grand Banks cod fishery in 1992 left over 35,000 fishers and plant workers from more than 400 coastal communities without jobs.

Seafood is also an important source of protein and micronutrients. More than 3 billion people are dependent on the ocean for protein, with fish representing at least 20% of their daily animal protein intake.

![Image 8: Colourful coral with shoal of small fish and manta ray swimming above](https://www.msc.org/images/default-source/msc-english/content-banner/content-hero-images-1920px-x-1080px/rs14035_shutterstock_1088086100-1920.tmb-large1920.jpg?Status=Master&Culture=en&sfvrsn=23ce54a1_1)

The impact of overfishing on biodiversity
-----------------------------------------

Overfishing can affect biodiversity, habitats and ecosystems that make vital contributions to the climate, clean air, water and food.

Biodiversity impacts can take place not only through overfishing of fish stocks targeted for food, but also through overfishing of [species caught unintentionally.](https://www.msc.org/what-we-are-doing/oceans-at-risk/what-is-bycatch-and-how-can-it-be-managed) This can include endangered species such as some sharks, rays, and turtles. Removing sharks, for example, can have knock-on effects on their prey and species lower down the food chain, and negatively impact biodiversity.

What can be done about overfishing?
-----------------------------------

Stopping overfishing doesn’t mean stopping fishing altogether. Fisheries that are sustainably managed are better for the environment and more productive in the long-term.

If all fisheries were sustainably managed there would be [an estimated 16 million more tonnes of fish a year, which would yield enough protein for 72 million more people.](https://www.msc.org/media-centre/press-releases/press-release/world-missing-out-on-nutrition-for-72-million-due-to-overfishing)

As the world grapples with how to feed its growing population, [sustainably produced foods from the sea will have an increasingly important role to play.](https://www.msc.org/docs/default-source/default-document-library/media-centre/msc-blue-foods-report.pdf)

Despite the headline statistics on overfishing, there has been steady progress on the availability of sustainable seafood for consumers. 2019 figures in the FAO Blue Transformation report found that 82% of fish landings by volume come from sustainably managed stocks, an increase of nearly 4%. This means consumers are increasingly empowered to make sustainable choices, especially for popular species like tuna, pollock and anchoveta.

[![Image 9: Climate change and fishing](https://www.msc.org/ResourcePackages/MSC/assets/img/defaultListImg.jpg)](https://www.msc.org/what-we-are-doing/our-collective-impact/fisheries-improving)

[How MSC certified fisheries are improving](https://www.msc.org/what-we-are-doing/our-collective-impact/fisheries-improving)
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

As well as fishing healthy populations, fisheries must show they are managing their impacts on habitats and other marine species.

What is the MSC doing about overfishing?
----------------------------------------

The MSC was founded in response to overfishing and our [Fisheries Standard](https://www.msc.org/standards-and-certification/fisheries-standard) can play a major role in its prevention.

Almost a fifth of the globe’s world marine catch is certified or in assessment to the Standard. It is a globally recognised benchmark for seafood sustainability and reflects the most up-to-date understanding of internationally accepted fisheries science and management.

The MSC Fisheries Standard is a set of requirements that ensure fisheries aiming to be certified as environmentally sustainable only target stocks that are abundant and able to replenish themselves, and that fisheries minimise their impact on the wider marine ecosystem. The Standard also requires that fishing is well-managed, and fishers abide by the relevant local, national and international laws.

Assessments are carried out independently and audits are made every year to make sure requirements are being met.

[Recent MSC research found that fish stocks targeted by fisheries certified to the MSC Standard perform better on key sustainability benchmarks](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fmars.2022.818772/full) than those targeted by non-certified fisheries. These stocks are healthier, more abundant, and much less prone to overfishing.

#### Your Privacy

When you visit any website, it may store or retrieve information on your browser, mostly in the form of cookies. This information might be about you, your preferences or your device and is mostly used to make the site work as you expect it to. The information does not usually directly identify you, but it can give you a more personalized web experience. Because we respect your right to privacy, you can choose not to allow some types of cookies. Click on the different category headings to find out more and change our default settings. However, blocking some types of cookies may impact your experience of the site and the services we are able to offer.

#### Strictly Necessary Cookies

Always Active

These cookies are necessary for the website to function and cannot be switched off in our systems. They are usually only set in response to actions made by you which amount to a request for services, such as setting your privacy preferences, logging in or filling in forms. You can set your browser to block or alert you about these cookies, but some parts of the site will not then work. These cookies do not store any personally identifiable information.

#### Performance Cookies

- [x] Performance Cookies 

These cookies allow us to count visits and traffic sources so we can measure and improve the performance of our site. They help us to know which pages are the most and least popular and see how visitors move around the site. All information these cookies collect is aggregated and therefore anonymous. If you do not allow these cookies we will not know when you have visited our site, and will not be able to monitor its performance.

#### Functional Cookies

- [x] Functional Cookies 

These cookies enable the website to provide enhanced functionality and personalisation. They may be set by us or by third party providers whose services we have added to our pages. If you do not allow these cookies then some or all of these services may not function properly.

#### Targeting Cookies

- [x] Targeting Cookies 

These cookies may be set through our site by our advertising partners. They may be used by those companies to build a profile of your interests and show you relevant adverts on other sites. They do not store directly personal information, but are based on uniquely identifying your browser and internet device. If you do not allow these cookies, you will experience less targeted advertising.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Overfishing occurs when fish are caught faster than they can reproduce, leading to severe declines in fish populations and threatening marine biodiversity, impacting entire ecosystems.
Destructive fishing practices, such as bottom trawling, damage vital habitats like coral reefs and seagrass beds, compromising marine life and ecosystem health.
Rising ocean temperatures and acidification alter fish migratory patterns and breeding, disrupting traditional fishing grounds and exacerbating the challenges of sustainable fisheries management.
Pollution from plastics and chemicals contaminates marine habitats, affecting fish health and reproduction, while also posing risks to human health through seafood consumption.
